title: Der Schneider im Himmel
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 35. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The thirty-fifth tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 35), a short satirical legend of heaven. While the Lord walks in the heavenly garden with his apostles and saints, a lame tailor talks his way past Saint Peter at the gate, and — seated on the Lord’s own all-seeing throne — hurls the golden footstool down at an old woman he catches stealing; on his return the Lord rebukes him, for if He punished every sin the moment it happened, nothing in heaven would be left. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, Thüre, Thor, gieng, ward, mußte, wußte, thun/that, gestolen and werth.

sentences.t: Es trug sich zu, daß der liebe Gott an einem schönen Tag in dem himmlischen Garten sich ergehen wollte und alle Apostel und Heiligen mit nahm, also daß niemand mehr im Himmel blieb als der heilige Petrus.
sentences.l: It came about that the dear God wanted one fine day to take a stroll in the heavenly garden and took all the apostles and saints along with him, so that no one remained in heaven any more but Saint Peter.
sentences.i: It came to pass that one fine day the dear Lord wished to take a walk in the heavenly garden, and took all the apostles and saints along with him, so that no one was left in heaven but Saint Peter.
sentences.n: “trug sich zu” is the separable sich zutragen (‘to come about, happen’), the solemn fairy-tale opener you also meet in Rapunzel. “sich ergehen” = to take a leisurely walk. “der heilige Petrus” = Saint Peter — heilig (‘holy’) before a name makes a saint.

sentences.t: Der Herr hatte ihm befohlen während seiner Abwesenheit niemand einzulassen, Petrus stand also an der Pforte und hielt Wache.
sentences.l: The Lord had commanded him to let no one in during his absence; Peter therefore stood at the gate and kept watch.
sentences.i: The Lord had ordered him to admit no one during his absence, so Peter stood at the gate and kept watch.
sentences.n: “befehlen” (‘to command’) takes the dative of the person: ihm, ‘him.’ “einlassen” is separable (‘to let in’), here as the zu-infinitive einzulassen with zu wedged into the verb. “Wache halten” = to keep watch.

sentences.t: Nicht lange so klopfte jemand an.
sentences.l: Not long, then someone knocked.
sentences.i: Before long, someone knocked.
sentences.n: “Nicht lange, so …” is the old resumptive frame, ‘it was not long before …’ “klopfte an” is the separable anklopfen (‘to knock’).

sentences.t: Petrus fragte wer da wäre und was er wollte.
sentences.l: Peter asked who was there and what he wanted.
sentences.n: “wäre” is subjunctive II in the indirect question — German shifts reported speech into the subjunctive, a pattern that runs through the whole tale.

sentences.t: „Ich bin ein armer ehrlicher Schneider,“ antwortete eine feine Stimme, „der um Einlaß bittet.“
sentences.l: “I am a poor honest tailor,” answered a thin voice, “who asks for admittance.”
sentences.i: “I am a poor, honest tailor,” answered a thin little voice, “begging to be let in.”
sentences.n: “um etwas bitten” = to ask for something (“um Einlaß bitten,” to ask for admittance). “Einlaß” is the 1857 spelling of Einlass. A “feine Stimme” is a fine, thin voice — the same phrase the smallest kid uses in Der Wolf und die sieben Geislein.

sentences.t: „Ja, ehrlich,“ sagte Petrus, „wie der Dieb am Galgen, du hast lange Finger gemacht und den Leuten das Tuch abgezwickt.
sentences.l: “Yes, honest,” said Peter, “like the thief on the gallows; you have made long fingers and snipped the cloth off folk.
sentences.i: “Honest, yes,” said Peter, “honest as the thief on the gallows — you have had light fingers and pinched people’s cloth.
sentences.n: “lange Finger machen” is an idiom for thieving, ‘to have light fingers.’ “den Leuten das Tuch abgezwickt” — dative den Leuten (‘from the people’) plus abzwicken, ‘to pinch/snip off’: tailors were proverbially suspected of keeping scraps of their customers’ cloth.

sentences.t: Du kommst nicht in den Himmel, der Herr hat mir verboten, so lange er draußen wäre, irgend jemand einzulassen.“
sentences.l: You do not come into heaven; the Lord has forbidden me, as long as he is outside, to let anyone at all in.”
sentences.i: You are not coming into heaven; the Lord has forbidden me to let anyone in at all while he is out.”
sentences.n: “irgend jemand” = anyone at all. “wäre” is again the reported-speech subjunctive (‘while he was out,’ given as the Lord’s own words).

sentences.t: „Seid doch barmherzig,“ rief der Schneider, „kleine Flicklappen, die von selbst vom Tisch herab fallen, sind nicht gestolen und nicht der Rede werth.
sentences.l: “Do be merciful,” cried the tailor, “little patching-scraps that fall down from the table of themselves are not stolen and not worth mentioning.
sentences.i: “Do be merciful,” cried the tailor; “little scraps of cloth that fall off the table by themselves are not stolen, and are not worth mentioning.
sentences.n: “Seid” is the old plural/polite imperative (the tailor addresses Peter with respectful Ihr). “gestolen” is the 1857 spelling of gestohlen (‘stolen’). “nicht der Rede werth” is a set phrase, ‘not worth mentioning’ (Rede = talk; werth carries the old -th of the edition).

sentences.t: Seht ich hinke und habe von dem Weg daher Blasen an den Füßen, ich kann unmöglich wieder umkehren.
sentences.l: See, I limp and have blisters on my feet from the way here; I can impossibly turn back again.
sentences.i: Look, I am lame and have blisters on my feet from the journey here — I simply cannot turn back.
sentences.n: “hinken” = to limp. “umkehren” (‘to turn back’) is separable. The tailor’s lameness is what finally wins Peter’s pity.

sentences.t: Laßt mich nur hinein, ich will alle schlechte Arbeit thun.
sentences.l: Just let me in; I will do all the low work.
sentences.i: Only let me in, and I will do all the dirty work.
sentences.n: “Laßt” is the old plural imperative of lassen (respectful Ihr again). “hinein” = in(ward); the verb of motion is dropped after it. “thun” is the 1857 spelling of tun (‘to do’).

sentences.t: Ich will die Kinder tragen, die Windeln waschen, die Bänke, darauf sie gespielt haben, säubern und abwischen, und ihre zerrissenen Kleider flicken.“
sentences.l: I will carry the children, wash the nappies, clean and wipe the benches on which they have played, and patch their torn clothes.”
sentences.i: I will carry the children, wash the swaddling-cloths, clean and wipe down the benches they have played on, and mend their torn clothes.”
sentences.n: “darauf … gespielt haben” = ‘on which they have played’ (darauf = ‘on it/that’). The tailor lists a servant’s chores; “flicken” (‘to patch, mend’) is his own trade turned to humble use.

sentences.t: Der heilige Petrus ließ sich aus Mitleiden bewegen, und öffnete dem lahmen Schneider die Himmelspforte so weit, daß er mit seinem dürren Leib hineinschlüpfen konnte.
sentences.l: Saint Peter let himself be moved out of pity, and opened the gate of heaven to the lame tailor so wide that he could slip in with his lean body.
sentences.i: Saint Peter let himself be moved by pity, and opened heaven’s gate to the lame tailor just wide enough for him to slip in with his scrawny frame.
sentences.n: “sich bewegen lassen” = to let oneself be swayed. “dem lahmen Schneider” is dative (‘for/to the lame tailor’). “hineinschlüpfen” (‘to slip inside’) is separable — a fittingly small verb for a lean tailor at a barely-open gate.

sentences.t: Er mußte sich in einen Winkel hinter die Thüre setzen, und sollte sich da still und ruhig verhalten, damit ihn der Herr wenn er zurückkäme, nicht bemerkte und zornig würde.
sentences.l: He had to seat himself in a corner behind the door, and was to keep himself still and quiet there, so that the Lord, when he came back, would not notice him and grow angry.
sentences.i: He had to sit down in a corner behind the door and keep still and quiet there, so that the Lord, when he returned, would not notice him and grow angry.
sentences.n: “sich verhalten” = to conduct oneself, keep (still). “Thüre” is the 1857 spelling of Türe (‘door’). “zurückkäme” is subjunctive II of the separable zurückkommen (‘if/when he came back’), the hypothetical the tailor is warned against.

sentences.t: Der Schneider gehorchte, als aber der heilige Petrus einmal zur Thüre hinaus trat, stand er auf, gieng voll Neugierde in allen Winkeln des Himmels herum und besah sich die Gelegenheit.
sentences.l: The tailor obeyed; but when Saint Peter once stepped out of the door, he stood up, went full of curiosity around in all the corners of heaven, and looked over the premises.
sentences.i: The tailor obeyed; but once, when Saint Peter stepped out of the door, he got up, wandered all over every corner of heaven out of curiosity, and looked the place over.
sentences.n: “stand auf” (aufstehen) and “gieng herum” (herumgehen) are separable. “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (‘went’). “sich die Gelegenheit besehen” = to look over the place / the lie of the land (Gelegenheit here in the old sense of ‘premises, layout’).

sentences.t: Endlich kam er zu einem Platz, da standen viele schöne und köstliche Stühle und in der Mitte ein ganz goldener Sessel, der mit glänzenden Edelsteinen besetzt war; er war auch viel höher als die übrigen Stühle, und ein goldener Fußschemel stand davor.
sentences.l: At last he came to a place; there stood many beautiful and precious chairs, and in the middle an all-golden armchair that was studded with glittering jewels; it was also much higher than the other chairs, and a golden footstool stood before it.
sentences.i: At last he came to a place where there stood many fine, costly chairs, and in the middle an all-golden throne studded with glittering jewels; it was also much higher than the other chairs, and a golden footstool stood before it.
sentences.n: Heaven’s furnishings are imagined as a lordly hall: “Sessel” is the grand armchair or throne (higher than the plain Stühle), with a “Fußschemel” (footstool) before it. “besetzt” (‘set, studded,’ past part. of besetzen) takes mit for the jewels.

sentences.t: Es war aber der Sessel, auf welchem der Herr saß, wenn er daheim war, und von welchem er alles sehen konnte, was auf Erden geschah.
sentences.l: But it was the chair on which the Lord sat when he was at home, and from which he could see everything that happened on earth.
sentences.i: But it was the very seat on which the Lord sat when he was at home, and from which he could see all that happened on earth.
sentences.n: “auf welchem … von welchem” = ‘on which … from which,’ the more formal relative welcher. “auf Erden” is an old fixed dative (‘on earth’) with no article, chiefly biblical and liturgical.

sentences.t: Der Schneider stand still und sah den Sessel eine gute Weile an, denn er gefiel ihm besser als alles andere.
sentences.l: The tailor stood still and looked at the chair a good while, for it pleased him better than everything else.
sentences.i: The tailor stood still and gazed at the seat a good while, for it pleased him more than anything else.
sentences.n: “sah … an” is separable ansehen (‘to look at’). “gefiel ihm” — gefallen takes the dative: it pleased him (ihm), i.e. he liked it.

sentences.t: Endlich konnte er den Vorwitz nicht bezähmen, stieg hinauf und setzte sich in den Sessel.
sentences.l: At last he could not curb his forwardness, climbed up, and seated himself in the chair.
sentences.i: At last he could not restrain his prying curiosity, climbed up, and sat down in the seat.
sentences.n: “der Vorwitz” = impudent, meddlesome curiosity — the forwardness that oversteps its place, exactly the tailor’s besetting fault. “bezähmen” = to curb, master. “stieg hinauf” (hinaufsteigen) is separable.

sentences.t: Da sah er alles was auf Erden geschah, und bemerkte eine alte häßliche Frau, die an einem Bach stand und wusch, und zwei Schleier heimlich bei Seite that.
sentences.l: There he saw everything that happened on earth, and noticed an old ugly woman who stood at a brook and washed, and secretly put two veils aside.
sentences.i: Now he saw everything that was happening on earth, and noticed an ugly old woman standing at a brook washing, who secretly set two veils aside.
sentences.n: “that” is the 1857 spelling of tat (‘did, put’), from thun. “bei Seite thun” = to put aside, here to filch. The tailor, given the Lord’s own all-seeing seat, uses it at once to spy out a petty theft.

sentences.t: Der Schneider erzürnte sich bei diesem Anblicke so sehr, daß er den goldenen Fußschemel ergriff und durch den Himmel auf die Erde hinab nach der alten Diebin warf.
sentences.l: The tailor grew so angry at this sight that he seized the golden footstool and threw it through heaven down to the earth at the old thief-woman.
sentences.i: The tailor was so enraged at the sight that he seized the golden footstool and hurled it through heaven, down to earth, at the old thieving woman.
sentences.n: “sich erzürnen” = to grow angry (reflexive). “bei diesem Anblicke” has the old dative -e (Anblick, ‘sight’). “Diebin” is the feminine of Dieb (‘thief’). His indignation is real, but his remedy — flinging heaven’s furniture at a sinner — is the joke the Lord will answer.

sentences.t: Da er aber den Schemel nicht wieder herauf holen konnte, so schlich er sich sachte aus dem Sessel weg, setzte sich an seinen Platz hinter die Thüre und that als ob er kein Wasser getrübt hätte.
sentences.l: But since he could not fetch the stool up again, he crept softly away out of the chair, sat himself in his place behind the door, and acted as if he had muddied no water.
sentences.i: But since he could not fetch the stool back up, he softly crept away from the seat, sat down in his place behind the door, and acted as if he had done nothing at all.
sentences.n: “Da … so …” here is causal, ‘since … , …’ “tun, als ob …” = to act as if. “kein Wasser getrübt haben” is an idiom, ‘to look as if one could not muddy water,’ i.e. to feign perfect innocence; getrübt is the past part. of trüben (‘to cloud, muddy’).

sentences.t: Als der Herr und Meister mit dem himmlischen Gefolge wieder zurückkam, ward er zwar den Schneider hinter der Thüre nicht gewahr, als er sich aber auf seinen Sessel setzte, mangelte der Schemel.
sentences.l: When the Lord and Master came back again with the heavenly retinue, he indeed did not become aware of the tailor behind the door; but when he sat himself on his chair, the stool was lacking.
sentences.i: When the Lord and Master returned with his heavenly train, he did not, it is true, notice the tailor behind the door; but when he sat down on his seat, the footstool was missing.
sentences.n: “zurückkam” is separable zurückkommen (‘came back’). “ward … gewahr” — “jemandes gewahr werden” = to become aware of someone; ward is the archaic preterite of werden. “mangelte” (mangeln) = was lacking, was missing. “das Gefolge” = retinue, train of followers.

sentences.t: Er fragte den heiligen Petrus wo der Schemel hingekommen wäre, der wußte es nicht.
sentences.l: He asked Saint Peter where the stool had got to; he did not know it.
sentences.i: He asked Saint Peter where the footstool had got to, but Peter did not know.
sentences.n: “hingekommen wäre” — the subjunctive of the reported question (‘where it had got to’). “der wußte es nicht” — the demonstrative der (‘he, that one’) points back to Peter; wußte is the 1857 spelling of wusste.

sentences.t: Da fragte er weiter ob er jemand hereingelassen hätte.
sentences.l: Then he asked further whether he had let anyone in.
sentences.n: “hereingelassen” is the past part. of the separable hereinlassen (‘to let in’). “hätte … hereingelassen” is again reported-speech subjunctive.

sentences.t: „Ich weiß niemand,“ antwortete Petrus „der da gewesen wäre, als ein lahmer Schneider, der noch hinter der Thüre sitzt.“
sentences.l: “I know of no one,” answered Peter, “who was here, but a lame tailor who still sits behind the door.”
sentences.i: “I know of no one,” answered Peter, “who has been here except a lame tailor who is still sitting behind the door.”
sentences.n: “als” here means ‘but, except’ (‘no one … except’), an older use. “gewesen wäre” is once more the reported-speech subjunctive; “sitzt,” by contrast, is plain indicative — Peter states the tailor’s present sitting as fact.

sentences.t: Da ließ der Herr den Schneider vor sich treten und fragte ihn ob er den Schemel weggenommen und wo er ihn hingethan hätte.
sentences.l: Then the Lord had the tailor step before him and asked him whether he had taken the stool away and where he had put it.
sentences.i: Then the Lord had the tailor come before him and asked him whether he had taken the footstool and where he had put it.
sentences.n: “ließ … treten” — lassen + infinitive, ‘to have/make (someone) step.’ “hingethan” is the 1857 spelling of hingetan, past part. of the separable hinthun (‘to put, place’). One shared hätte governs both clauses (‘had taken … had put’).

sentences.t: „O Herr,“ antwortete der Schneider freudig, „ich habe ihn im Zorne hinab auf die Erde nach einem alten Weibe geworfen, das ich bei der Wäsche zwei Schleier stehlen sah.“
sentences.l: “O Lord,” answered the tailor joyfully, “I have thrown it down to the earth in anger at an old woman whom I saw stealing two veils at her washing.”
sentences.i: “O Lord,” answered the tailor gladly, “in my anger I threw it down to earth at an old woman whom I saw stealing two veils at the washing.”
sentences.n: “freudig” (‘joyfully’) — the tailor is proud of his zeal, blind to his offense. “Weibe” has the old dative -e (Weib, plainly ‘woman’). “bei der Wäsche” = at the washing/laundry. “sah … stehlen” — sehen + bare infinitive, ‘saw (her) steal.’

sentences.t: „O du Schalk,“ sprach der Herr, „wollt ich richten wie du richtest, wie meinst du daß es dir schon längst ergangen wäre?
sentences.l: “O you rogue,” spoke the Lord, “were I to judge as you judge, how do you think it would long since have gone for you?
sentences.i: “O you rogue,” said the Lord, “if I judged as you judge, how do you suppose it would have fared with you long ago?
sentences.n: The tale’s hinge — divine patience. “wollt ich richten” is a conditional by inversion (‘if I were to judge’). “wie es dir ergangen wäre” — ergehen (‘to fare, turn out’) with the dative dir: ‘how it would have gone for you.’ The Lord’s point: judged at once, no one would survive.

sentences.t: ich hätte schon lange keine Stühle, Bänke, Sessel, ja keine Ofengabel mehr hier gehabt, sondern alles nach den Sündern hinabgeworfen.
sentences.l: I would long since have had no chairs, benches, armchairs, indeed no oven-fork here any more, but would have thrown everything down at the sinners.
sentences.i: I would long since have had no chairs, benches, seats, not even an oven-fork left up here, but would have flung everything down at the sinners.
sentences.n: The Lord extends the tailor’s logic to absurdity: to punish every sin at once would strip heaven bare. “die Ofengabel” (‘oven-fork,’ a hearth tool) is the homely last item — nothing would be left. “hinabgeworfen” is the past part. of separable hinabwerfen; “den Sündern” is dative plural (‘at the sinners’).

sentences.t: Fortan kannst du nicht mehr im Himmel bleiben, sondern mußt wieder hinaus vor das Thor: da sieh zu wo du hinkommst.
sentences.l: Henceforth you can no longer stay in heaven, but must go out again before the gate: there see to where you get to.
sentences.i: From now on you can stay in heaven no longer, but must go out again beyond the gate — and there see for yourself where you end up.
sentences.n: “fortan” = henceforth. “Thor” is the 1857 spelling of Tor (‘gate’). “sieh zu” (zusehen, ‘to see to it’) is a separable imperative. “wo du hinkommst” — hinkommen (‘to get to, end up’) is separable; the hin splits off (wo … hin).

sentences.t: Hier soll niemand strafen, denn ich allein, der Herr.“
sentences.l: Here no one shall punish but I alone, the Lord.”
sentences.n: “denn” here means ‘but, except’ (an older usage, not ‘for’). The moral is stated flat: judgment is reserved to God alone — the tailor’s sin was usurping it.

sentences.t: Petrus mußte den Schneider wieder hinaus vor den Himmel bringen, und weil er zerrissene Schuhe hatte und die Füße voll Blasen, nahm er einen Stock in die Hand, und zog nach Warteinweil, wo die frommen Soldaten sitzen und sich lustig machen.
sentences.l: Peter had to bring the tailor out again before heaven; and because he had torn shoes and his feet full of blisters, he took a stick in his hand and set off for Wait-a-while, where the pious soldiers sit and make merry.
sentences.i: Peter had to lead the tailor back out before heaven; and because he had torn shoes and his feet were full of blisters, he took a staff in hand and set off for Wait-a-while, where the pious soldiers sit and make merry.
sentences.n: “Warteinweil” is a joke place-name — ‘Wait-a-while,’ an imaginary land of idlers where cast-offs go to loiter. “zog” (ziehen) here = to travel, set off. “sich lustig machen” = to make merry, amuse oneself. The dry ending packs the presumptuous tailor off to a fool’s paradise.
